metadata.title: Modeling and measuring class conflict in Russia’s regions

abstract: This article tests whether latent class conflict exists in Russia. It does so by theorizing that if class conflict exists, it should be reflected in the tax policy. The article constructs an original formal model of the authoritarian tax policy choice, finding equilibria in which the local government takes into account public sentiment even in the absence of elections, in an effect that resembles a market. This public sentiment is operationalized by performing a large-n latent Dirichlet allocation topic analysis of Russian regional newspaper articles dealing with the middle and working classes. In empirically testing its formal model, this article finds evidence that certain tax policy is driven by class conflict.

sections.paper_id: Wilson - 2019 - Modeling and measuring class conflict in Russia’s regions
paragraphs: Russian regional taxation is a system ripe for scientific examination, especially prior to its 2006 consolidation. 1 With widespread tax evasion and little regional variation in local tax powers, the sources of revenue nonetheless vary widely regionally, in ways unexplained by the usual suspects of industrial or economic variance. This article argues that variance in class identity can explain these outcomes, and that even in authoritarian contexts, the middle and working classes can implicitly influence policies over which local authorities have control. A "hidden market" exists, deriving from competition between classes whose tax preferences will be prioritized by the local government. This market is hidden because it is outside of formal political institutions.

I construct a formal model explaining the effects of class pressure on the local legislature responsible for setting tax burdens, operationalize class identity through an automated content analysis of regional newspapers, and test the model's predictions against actual regional policy. This approach has the added value of also providing a detailed analysis of what values and culture are associated with the middle and working classes in Russia, with a minimum of a priori assumptions.

sections.paper_id: Wilson - 2019 - Modeling and measuring class conflict in Russia’s regions
Wilson - 2019 - Modeling and measuring class conflict in Russia’s regions
paragraphs: There are two basic approaches to talking about class, which I call structural and cultural.

The structural approach treats the class as a distinct socioeconomic entity that can be quantified through structural measures, and which may act as a cohesive, if not necessarily self-aware actor capable of causing societal change (Remington, 2011). This approach has blossomed from roots in political economy, modeling (whether formally or not) individuals as agents acting in their own economic interest. I label this approach structural precisely because its theories can generally be operationalized through structural measures such as education level, income, and occupational type.

The structural approach can be seen from a certain perspective as a topdown approach, in that it establishes a definition for the class (whether middle or otherwise) and then proceeds to analyze it in those terms. This approach produces a variety of widely divergent theories, from classic Marxism, to Barrington Moore's "no bourgeois, no democracy," to Seymour Lipset's contention that building a middle class is the sine-qua-non for democratization (Lipset, 1959;Moore, 1967Moore, [1966]]).

In contemporary writing, work focusing on class from the structural perspective is divided into two loose categories, depending on the particular broad definition of class used. Thomas Remington (2011) has labeled these the "agent of history" and "social stratum" definitions, while Resnick and Wolff (2003) dubbed them the "property-and-power" and "wealthand-education-and-prestige" definitions. Note that such categorizations are not cut and dry, with most scholars bridging the gaps with complementary approaches.

The first of these definitions ("agent of history"/"property-and-power") treats class as an explicitly economic phenomenon, with other elements subordinated to those emerging from economic power relations. Two schools of thought dominate this literature: the neo-Weberian and the neo-Marxist, spearheaded, respectively, by John Goldthorpe and Erik Olin Wright. Wright's neo-Marxist approach adds nuance to Marxism's dialectic foundations with the principle of class locations, mutually exclusive exploitative positions that operate on several dimensions (such as level of workplace authority) in addition to property ownership. This allows a theoretical multiplicity of classes, including a stable middle class, while retaining the core Marxist contribution of dichotomous exploitation. Goldthorpe's neo-Weberian approach relies on the Goldthorpe class schema, which establishes class boundaries on the basis of occupational types, deemphasizing ownership in favor of variation in life chances as the primary class boundary. However, while theoretically distinct, when mapped onto the structural elements necessary to measure them, these two schools of thought result in relatively similar measures.

The applied literature following these two theoretical schools either perform original data collection to measure the structural variables of theoretical interest, or use proxy variables (such as state classification of job types and income level) that are more readily accessible from state statistical agencies. A notable sub-literature is those works that have drawn on Wright's own Comparative Project on Class Structure and Class Consciousness (Wright, 1989). This project surveyed households in 10 developed countries over the period of 1980-1987 in order to generate a database of class locations. Additional work has continued to draw on these data, including with regard to gender (Arrighi and Maume, 2000;Baxter and Kane, 1995;Jones, 2001;Khazzoom, 1997), inequality (Blau and Kahn, 1996), and class mobility (Kingston, 1996). Wright's work has been the basis of a large-n survey project on class in Russia, performing surveys in 1988, 1998, and 2007(Blom, 1991;Nikula, 2002;Nikula and Chernysh, 2010).

In addition, a significant sub-literature has grown around Erikson andGoldthorpe's (1992a, 1992b) CASIN Project. These works have touched on inequality (Thorslund and Lundberg, 1994), social mobility (Erikson et al., 2012;Torche, 2005), and even social mobility in Russia in particular (Gerber and Hout, 2004). Natalia Tikhonova's work over the past decade on class in Russia has drawn primarily from this neo-Weberian framework (Tikhonova, 2008;Tikhonova and Mareeva, 2009).

Intriguingly, research into class during the Soviet period found that when approached using surveys instead of state classification, Soviet society could largely be classified into six to eight categories that roughly mapped onto the classification schemes of both Wright and Goldthorpe, despite taking place outside of a capitalist context (Shkaratan et al., 1980;Shkaratan and Rukavishnikov, 1986;Swafford, 1987;Zimmerman, 1987).

The second definition used in the structural approach ("social stratum"/"wealth-and-education-and-prestige") is simpler in theoretical terms, assigning a particular class a definition in terms of measures such as level of income or level of education. A plethora of such projects have evaluated the Russian middle class in particular over the past decade, including work by the Independent Institute of Social Policy in Moscow (Maleva, 2008), The Institute of Sociology of the Academy of Sciences (Tikhonova, 2008), the Levada Center (Khakhulina, 2008), and the Russia Longitudinal Monitoring Survey (Yudina, 2008). Individual projects have also proliferated, using a variety of tweaks to surveys and variations on the basic framework of what constitutes the middle class (Gorshkov and Tikhonova, 2008;Shkaratan, 2009). Each of these works contains valuable analysis of what proportion of Russians fit which components of the middle class and in what combinations (see Remington (2011) for a detailed breakdown of the findings, strengths, and weaknesses of each of these).

This body of literature does not reach a consensus about the size and strength of a Russian middle class. If anything, the literature demonstrates that the results of a structural approach are largely dependent on how the initial measurement criteria are defined. Although this is the case to a certain degree with any measurement, in this case it seems to be an indication that the concept of the middle class is under-theorized, in that there is little consensus about what the middle class should look like structurally. Resnick and Wolff (2003) argue that the literature of class analysis in general is plagued by a definitional problem in which "one or another class definition and deployment is used as if it were the only or the ultimate or the universally agreed kind of class analysis." They argue that work utilizing class analysis should establish upfront a concrete definition of class and explain why that definition in particular is the superior one to use in the context of the work. This is good general advice for research, but in the case of Russia and class, the inconsistent definitions are also a function of uncertainty over what exactly the middle class is in the Russian context. This is exacerbated by the fact that in the Russian context the middle class has been a shifting concept for at least the past three decades. During the late Soviet era, a "proto-middleclass" of well-educated state employees existed, and although arguments persist over what role it played in the downfall of the Soviet Union, it is clear that the interests of that middle class were at stark odds with the emergence of a capitalist middle class during the early 1990s (Remington, 2011).

After the fall of the Soviet Union, the proportion of the population selfidentifying as middle class plummeted, even while splitting into two discrete groups: those dependent on the state for employment and those in the private sector. These subclasses have starkly different socioeconomic interests, despite being similar in structural measures, occupational types, and class locations. Even after 20 years, the proportion of the middle class employed by the state is still around 50% in Russia (Shkaratan, 2009). 2 This results in a certain fluidity of definition with regard to class in Russia, and segues nicely into the cultural approach to class. The cultural approach eschews defining classes exclusively in structural terms in favor of defining each as a specific "package of values and behaviors" (Remington, 2011), or as a "normative model" (Melin and Salmenniemi, 2013).

This approach is not just important academically, but is especially interesting to policymakers, who are often invested in the development of a middle class in states like Russia, but rarely because of an unadulterated desire to see education and income levels rise. The consistent calls for the growth of a middle class may be grounded in modernization or social capital theory, but their basic logic is founded on the notion that education and income create a middle class, which has values conducive to policies that we like. The question of whether these things actually do produce the values in question is the subject of several decades of literature outside the scope of this conservation. Proponents of building democratic institutions are not alone in their praise of middle-class virtue. Remington (2011) notes that Putin, among others in the upper echelons of his regime, has repeatedly expressed the view that the virtues traditionally associated with the middle class -a well-developed work ethic, the habit of saving and investing for the future, modesty in consumption, an orientation toward stability and security -are normatively desirable and important as policy objectives.

The cultural approach can either define the set of values in question and attempt to measure them directly via tools like surveys or content analysis, or it can use such tools in order to try to evaluate what the values associated with the middle class are in a certain society. Both techniques have their merits in different contexts.

In the Russian context, the first approach has operated hand-in-hand with the structural surveys discussed earlier. The middle class is defined by a certain level of income and education, and then research is conducted into how associated certain normative values are with that group. Several authors present excellent reviews of this literature (Melin and Salmenniemi, 2013;Remington, 2011;Tikhonova and Mareeva, 2009). In addition to works measuring more classic "middle-class values," this literature includes small-n indepth interviews of opinions of the middle class (Diligenskiy, 2002), surveys on the preference of equality versus opportunity (Gorshkov and Tikhonova, 2008), surveys on economic self-confidence (Kuzina, 2009;Maleva, 2008), and surveys on the self-perceived value to society (Patico, 2008). A cornerstone of many of these surveys is the tracking of whether individuals selfidentify as middle class, independently from whether they meet the structural or cultural definitions of the middle class (Maleva, 2007;Yudina, 2008).

These surveys form a bridge to the body of work on social identity, in that they are identifying both structural and cultural elements that combine in complex ways to form an individual's relationship with a large group of which she is a member. This highlights the distinction between class and class identity. Membership in a class might be strictly a structural artifact (depending on one's definition), that is, membership depends solely on whether an individual has the appropriate income, education, or whatever other variable is used to define that membership. However, class identity depends explicitly both on being part of that structure and on subscribing to some cultural content associated with that structure. The cultural approach is inextricably linked with class identity because of its focus on defining and measuring the normative values of class.

This article draws upon the framework for social identities developed by Abdelal et al. (2009), which is based on two dimensions: content and contestation. Content is the gestalt beliefs, values, behaviors, and norms that are associated with a given social identity, while contestation is the level of disagreement in a group over that gestalt. In a sense, content is the mean, while contestation is the shape of the distribution surrounding that mean. The cultural approach to studying the class fits perfectly into this framework, the surrounding literature of which gives us a selection of tools to use, to apply specifically to the Russian case. While surveys and interviews have been widely deployed to analyze the Russian middle class, content analysis has been a neglected tool in this context.

Content analysis has a long history in the social sciences, especially with the analysis of newspapers in order to tease out a variety of cultural variables both qualitatively and quantitatively. Studies using content analysis are too innumerable to list here, but see Herrera and Braumoeller (2004) for a brief summary of several points of view, and Abdelal et al. (2009) for articles by several authors on different approaches to measuring social identity through content analysis, in addition to extensive general guides by Krippendorff (2012) and Neuendorf (2002). One of the strengths of content analysis relative to other methods is the ability to use a bottom-up approach. That is, by reading every article that talks about class in Russian newspapers, we will come away with an idea of what the cultural content of class is, without having framed our answers by our definition.

Given the uncertainty over what the middle class in Russia really is, over both its structural size and cultural content, this article sees an opportunity to use computer-automated content analysis on Russian newspapers. This approach can construct a picture of that "package of values and behaviors" that defines the Russian working and middle classes, with minimal a priori assumptions about the nature of that cultural content.

A plethora of such projects have evaluated the Russian middle class in particular over the past decade, including work by the Independent Institute of Social Policy in Moscow (Maleva, 2008), The Institute of Sociology of the Academy of Sciences (Tikhonova, 2008), the Levada Center (Khakhulina, 2008), and the Russia Longitudinal Monitoring Survey (Yudina, 2008). Individual projects have also proliferated, using a variety of tweaks to surveys and variations on the basic framework of what constitutes the middle class (Gorshkov and Tikhonova, 2008;Shkaratan, 2009). Each of these works contains valuable analysis of what proportion of Russians fit which components of the middle class and in what combinations (see Remington (2011) for a detailed breakdown of the findings, strengths, and weaknesses of each of these).

This body of literature does not reach a consensus about the size and strength of a Russian middle class. If anything, the literature demonstrates that the results of a structural approach are largely dependent on how the initial measurement criteria are defined. Although this is the case to a certain degree with any measurement, in this case it seems to be an indication that the concept of the middle class is under-theorized, in that there is little consensus about what the middle class should look like structurally. Resnick and Wolff (2003) argue that the literature of class analysis in general is plagued by a definitional problem in which "one or another class definition and deployment is used as if it were the only or the ultimate or the universally agreed kind of class analysis." They argue that work utilizing class analysis should establish upfront a concrete definition of class and explain why that definition in particular is the superior one to use in the context of the work. This is good general advice for research, but in the case of Russia and class, the inconsistent definitions are also a function of uncertainty over what exactly the middle class is in the Russian context. This is exacerbated by the fact that in the Russian context the middle class has been a shifting concept for at least the past three decades. During the late Soviet era, a "proto-middleclass" of well-educated state employees existed, and although arguments persist over what role it played in the downfall of the Soviet Union, it is clear that the interests of that middle class were at stark odds with the emergence of a capitalist middle class during the early 1990s (Remington, 2011).

After the fall of the Soviet Union, the proportion of the population selfidentifying as middle class plummeted, even while splitting into two discrete groups: those dependent on the state for employment and those in the private sector. These subclasses have starkly different socioeconomic interests, despite being similar in structural measures, occupational types, and class locations. Even after 20 years, the proportion of the middle class employed by the state is still around 50% in Russia (Shkaratan, 2009). 2 This results in a certain fluidity of definition with regard to class in Russia, and segues nicely into the cultural approach to class. The cultural approach eschews defining classes exclusively in structural terms in favor of defining each as a specific "package of values and behaviors" (Remington, 2011), or as a "normative model" (Melin and Salmenniemi, 2013).

This approach is not just important academically, but is especially interesting to policymakers, who are often invested in the development of a middle class in states like Russia, but rarely because of an unadulterated desire to see education and income levels rise. The consistent calls for the growth of a middle class may be grounded in modernization or social capital theory, but their basic logic is founded on the notion that education and income create a middle class, which has values conducive to policies that we like. The question of whether these things actually do produce the values in question is the subject of several decades of literature outside the scope of this conservation. Proponents of building democratic institutions are not alone in their praise of middle-class virtue. Remington (2011) notes that Putin, among others in the upper echelons of his regime, has repeatedly expressed the view that the virtues traditionally associated with the middle class -a well-developed work ethic, the habit of saving and investing for the future, modesty in consumption, an orientation toward stability and security -are normatively desirable and important as policy objectives.

The cultural approach can either define the set of values in question and attempt to measure them directly via tools like surveys or content analysis, or it can use such tools in order to try to evaluate what the values associated with the middle class are in a certain society. Both techniques have their merits in different contexts.

In the Russian context, the first approach has operated hand-in-hand with the structural surveys discussed earlier. The middle class is defined by a certain level of income and education, and then research is conducted into how associated certain normative values are with that group. Several authors present excellent reviews of this literature (Melin and Salmenniemi, 2013;Remington, 2011;Tikhonova and Mareeva, 2009). In addition to works measuring more classic "middle-class values," this literature includes small-n indepth interviews of opinions of the middle class (Diligenskiy, 2002), surveys on the preference of equality versus opportunity (Gorshkov and Tikhonova, 2008), surveys on economic self-confidence (Kuzina, 2009;Maleva, 2008), and surveys on the self-perceived value to society (Patico, 2008). A cornerstone of many of these surveys is the tracking of whether individuals selfidentify as middle class, independently from whether they meet the structural or cultural definitions of the middle class (Maleva, 2007;Yudina, 2008).

These surveys form a bridge to the body of work on social identity, in that they are identifying both structural and cultural elements that combine in complex ways to form an individual's relationship with a large group of which she is a member. This highlights the distinction between class and class identity. Membership in a class might be strictly a structural artifact (depending on one's definition), that is, membership depends solely on whether an individual has the appropriate income, education, or whatever other variable is used to define that membership. However, class identity depends explicitly both on being part of that structure and on subscribing to some cultural content associated with that structure. The cultural approach is inextricably linked with class identity because of its focus on defining and measuring the normative values of class.

This article draws upon the framework for social identities developed by Abdelal et al. (2009), which is based on two dimensions: content and contestation. Content is the gestalt beliefs, values, behaviors, and norms that are associated with a given social identity, while contestation is the level of disagreement in a group over that gestalt. In a sense, content is the mean, while contestation is the shape of the distribution surrounding that mean. The cultural approach to studying the class fits perfectly into this framework, the surrounding literature of which gives us a selection of tools to use, to apply specifically to the Russian case. While surveys and interviews have been widely deployed to analyze the Russian middle class, content analysis has been a neglected tool in this context.

Content analysis has a long history in the social sciences, especially with the analysis of newspapers in order to tease out a variety of cultural variables both qualitatively and quantitatively. Studies using content analysis are too innumerable to list here, but see Herrera and Braumoeller (2004) for a brief summary of several points of view, and Abdelal et al. (2009) for articles by several authors on different approaches to measuring social identity through content analysis, in addition to extensive general guides by Krippendorff (2012) and Neuendorf (2002). One of the strengths of content analysis relative to other methods is the ability to use a bottom-up approach. That is, by reading every article that talks about class in Russian newspapers, we will come away with an idea of what the cultural content of class is, without having framed our answers by our definition.

Given the uncertainty over what the middle class in Russia really is, over both its structural size and cultural content, this article sees an opportunity to use computer-automated content analysis on Russian newspapers. This approach can construct a picture of that "package of values and behaviors" that defines the Russian working and middle classes, with minimal a priori assumptions about the nature of that cultural content.

sections.paper_id: Wilson - 2019 - Modeling and measuring class conflict in Russia’s regions
paragraphs: There is also a broad literature in political economy and formal theory dealing with decision making by rulers. These contributions can be divided into addressing either of two basic types of rulers: office-seeking and policyseeking. Both ideal types were traditionally developed in models of electoral competition, and differ based on the underlying source of the ruler's utility. The office-seeking type of politician has no preference over policy and derives utility instead from winning office. This model is best known as the Hotelling-Downs model (Downs, 1957;Hotelling, 1929). The policyseeking type of politician does not seek office for its own sake, but in order to implement the policy closest to her preferences (Wittman, 1973). There are many variations on these basic models, but they largely exist only in an electoral context. These models do not directly apply to this article's problem because I am modeling a system without multiple potential rulers. In other words, the tension of these models exists because there are different choices of ruler, and cannot survive when limited to a single ruler.

Models that apply to authoritarian contexts can be fit into these same two categories though. Models that favor the predatory state interpretation argue that policy equilibria derive from rulers attempting to maximize revenue, which can be understood as fundamentally policy-seeking behavior (Levi, 1989;Przeworski, 2003). On the other hand, models arguing that rulers aim to maintain their hold on power, are basically office-seeking (Acemoglu and Robinson, 2009;Mesquita et al., 2004). These models are friendlier to modeling authoritarian rulers, but rest on one of two tensions not present in this particular puzzle. Either politicians gain utility from more revenue or they gain utility from not being removed. Neither tension suits the regional authoritarian legislatures in Russia. As noted, the first tension does not apply, since such an actor does not necessarily have the power to modify the total revenue collected so much as decide from where that revenue will come. And the second tension is equally invalid in this case, since the local officials' decision making is simply not at a high enough level to be concerned with avoiding a coup.

A slightly different sub-literature is that involving the exploration of the nuances of authoritarian behavior. For example, Jennifer Gandhi's work analyzing the rationale behind nominally democratic trappings maintained by otherwise authoritarian societies (such as legislatures and elections) touches on exactly the sort of complexity of authoritarian behavior that this article seeks to tease out (Gandhi, 2010). In addition, the literature on tax policy and, specifically, on Russian tax policy also serves as a grounding for this article. Gehlbach's work on post-Communist taxation in particular serves as a starting point for this article's notion that the choice of tax policy is a great deal more nuanced than simply maximizing (Gehlbach, 2010).
This article contributes to this sub-literature by developing the strategic interaction between the local legislature and the populations from which they extract revenue.

sections.paper_id: Wilson - 2019 - Modeling and measuring class conflict in Russia’s regions
paragraphs: I model class conflict over tax policy as a one-shot game of three players: bureaucrat (as a stand-in for the local legislature or regional government in the case of Russia), the working class, and the middle class. The two classes act as black boxes, which have internally overcome their collective action problems. 3 The upper class is not included because the extrajudicial power of Russian elites suggests that their arrangements with authorities would be exogenous to strategic decision making among the lower classes and local bureaucrats, as they are part of the exogenous process assigning revenue quotas to the bureaucrats. Similarly, the federal government is not included because it is part of the exogenous process setting the bureaucrat's quota. 4 At the beginning of the game, the bureaucrat is exogenously assigned a revenue quota to be extracted from the lower classes, and then decides what proportion to extract from each. I designate the total revenue T, and the proportion from the middle and working classes as τ M and τ W . For simplicity, each of these variables is ∈ [0,1]. Furthermore, let us label the proportion of τ M with respect to T (i.e. τ M / T ) the tax bias, since this particular proportion will be used extensively throughout this article.

The subjective utility function of the bureaucrat derives from her unenviable position: caught between orders from above and friction from below. Thus, I model the bureaucrat as "hassle-avoiding," deriving no utility from the revenue itself, but only from how quiescent the population is. Since the bureaucrat has no control over her quota, she can be modeled as a classic fixed-output firm, producing one output (revenue T), one input (cash), acquired in different quantities (τ M and τ W ) from two suppliers (the middle and working classes). The solution concept is one of cost minimization, meaning that the bureaucrat selects τ M and τ W such that it costs her the least, while keeping the output T constant.

The middle and working classes are identical players, distinguished only by parameterization. The only action that either class can take is the decision to raise an outcry. This does not necessarily involve street protest, but implies the general noise and clamor of a group of individuals. Authoritarian rulers put the utmost value on keeping the population quiet. A group that is unhappy enough to start to raise noise is a potential threat to the regime, and bureaucrats on whose watch this happens tend to get fired. I label the general clamor raised by a particular group as shum (pronounced "shoom," due to the vagaries of Russian-to-English transliteration rules), a Russian word that summarizes a sort of general uproar by the population. For instance, Shakespeare's "Much Ado About Nothing" is translated into Russian as "Mnogo Shuma . . ." and Putin himself has directly referenced the outcry of the public in response to changes in tax policy as shum as recently as his June 2018 Direct Line appearance (TVC, 2018). Shum is defined in this article as the general level of noise, clamor, and outcry that a particular group is raising at a particular time. Investment in shum gives no direct benefit, only cost. The motivation behind investing in shum is that it increases the bureaucrat's cost of taxing that class and thus is a strategic action taken in order to reduce one's own taxation.

I designate S M and S W ∈ [0,1] as the level of shum of the middle and working classes, where zero corresponds to a completely quiescent population, while one corresponds to that group at its loudest (newspapers full of angry articles, people in the streets).
Thus, the bureaucrat's utility function U B , is

where p M (•) and p W (•) are the price functions that convert the shum of each class into a price for the bureaucrat. These price functions are similar, but differ in parameterization to reflect that the bureaucrat may care more about an uproar raised by one class or the other. I define α ∈ [0,∞) and β ∈ [0,∞) as the measure of how much the bureaucrat cares about middle-and working-class shum, respectively. So a bureaucrat with α = 0 does not care at all, and any level of shum would be insufficient to shift her policy choice. On the other hand, a bureaucrat with α =∞ values the opinion of the middle class so highly that any level of shum at all would be sufficient shift policy. Or in economic terms, it costs the population more to shift the price of a bureaucrat with low α or β. Now, consider the following functional forms for p M (•) and p W (•)

While these functional forms are constructed to be simple and tractable, they also accurately reflect a number of political realities relevant to the empirical context of the model. The inclusion of terms for τ M and τ W reflects the argument that even holding shum constant, the political price of taxation should be higher per unit of tax at higher tax rates. Also note that these functional forms implicitly bind prices such that p M and p W ∈ [0,1].

The effects of α and β are also quite interesting. When these parameters are greater than one, the price curve is concave with respect to shum, meaning that a small change in outcry has disproportionate effects on the political price commanded by that group. This is the case for an oligarchical group. When these parameters are less than one, the price curve is convex in shum, thus requiring a group to disproportionately raise an outcry in order to affect politics. This is the case for a repressed group. Values of exactly one reflect the price following shum in perfect proportionality, a situation one might describe as an ideal type of democracy. More precisely, these functional forms imply that democracy is the state of constant return on mobilization These relationships are illustrated in Figure 1.
The middle-and working-class utility functions follow, with the cost of shum for each set to the constants ω M and ω

This is the case for a repressed group. Values of exactly one reflect the price following shum in perfect proportionality, a situation one might describe as an ideal type of democracy. More precisely, these functional forms imply that democracy is the state of constant return on mobilization These relationships are illustrated in Figure 1.

The middle-and working-class utility functions follow, with the cost of shum for each set to the constants ω M and ω
sections.preview_head: I model class conflict over tax policy as a one-shot game of three players: bureaucrat (as a stand-in for the local legislature or regional government in the case of Russia), the working class, and the middle class. The two classes act as black boxes, which have internally overcome their collective action problems. 3 The upper class is not included because the extrajudicial power of Russian elites suggests that their arrangements with authorities would be exogenous to strategic decision making among the lower classes and local bureaucrats, as they are part of the exogenous process assigning revenue quotas to the bureaucrats. Similarly, the federal government is not included because it is part of the exogenous process setting the bureaucrat's quota. 4 At the beginning of the game, the bureaucrat is exogenously assigned a revenue quota to be extracted from the lower classes, and then decides what proportion to extract from each.

paragraphs: The game now has three players, with three utility functions, each of which are defined only in terms of exogenous parameters and the actions of the other players. Verifying that first-and second-order conditions allow a maximization, and using the solution concept of a partial equilibrium under perfect information, I derived the following equilibrium strategy for the bureaucrat, which maximizes her utility 5

Restricting attention to the bureaucrat's equilibrium taxation strategy, some interesting implications emerge from the comparative statics. 6 First, the burden of middle-class taxation is increasing in S W , indicating that as the level of working-class shum drops to zero, the middle class's tax burden should drop to zero. Second, τ M is decreasing in S M , so that if middle-class shum drops to zero, any investment in shum whatsoever by the working class will lead to the tax burden being borne entirely by the middle class. In addition, if the bureaucrat is equally biased toward the middle and working classes (in other words, if α = β) and both sides invest equally in shum (i.e. S M = S W ), then each side will bear exactly half the required tax burden.

sections.paper_id: Wilson - 2019 - Modeling and measuring class conflict in Russia’s regions
paragraphs: Prior to 2006, regional revenue in Russia was broken into seven different classifications (six taxes plus federal grants). The summary statistics of the proportion of regional revenue derived from these sources appear in Table 1. 7 The legal rates for these values were established federally, but much leeway existed in the collection efforts by local officials. The personal income tax and land tax had federally determined minimum and maximum values, with the precise value left to the discretion of regional authorities. For example, land tax rates varied based on the type of land: agricultural, recreational, and so on. Rules often distinguished between land held by different types of owners, and sometimes different levels of regional government (local or municipal) received some proportion of the regional rate. In addition, regional authorities controlled the periodic revaluation of lands, which has led to well-documented and systematic undervaluation of land by local assessors. In conjunction with widespread tax evasion, it is clear that while the tax code might only loosely approximate actual collection, the revenue collected is a direct measure of how much local officials squeeze from different targets.

These seven different classifications can be winnowed down a bit. First, the federal grants are outside any control of local bureaucrats, and do not represent money that they squeeze from the local population, so it can be discarded as a potential source of class conflict. Second, taxes upon resource and mineral extraction are not targeted at the middle or working class at all, but upon large corporations controlled by elites, and so is also not a potential source of class conflict. That leaves five taxes which have the potential to be a source of conflict between the middle and working classes, any pair of which can provide values for τ W and τ M for testing.

In theory, it makes sense that there should be tension between the land tax and the personal income tax, as those are the two that have de facto flexibility for the local authorities, and have clear preferences for the middle and working classes. That is, the working class would prefer a higher land tax as opposed to income tax as they have income but not land, while some portion of the middle class would be expected to hold the opposite preference, given their potential for land ownership as a component of class identity. 8 During the period of this study, from 2000 to 2004 the land tax was regionally administered while the income tax was federally managed. Local officials were allowed to set the land tax within a federally mandated range, which therefore puts it in the purview of regional officials (Bird and Slack, 2002). 9 The World Bank's 2003 report on regional taxation in Russia describes explicitly how the regional legislature "differentiates rates" based on types of land, distance from the center of the region, and a variety of other nuanced details (Timofeev, 2003). The lack of clearly defined property rights in Russia during this period increased the power that regional authorities directly had over the amount of revenue collected, even beyond that of the variance they themselves created through local regulation (Malme and Kalinina, 2001). Indeed, the complexity at the local level was such that local authorities could design land taxation of nearly arbitrary levels of complexity, such as in the city of Kostroma where 20 different land tax rates were established in the late-1990s across two forms of property and 10 zones just within the city itself (Wegren, 1997). The intertwined nature of entrepreneurs and local government officials meant that those responsible for implementing the laws that determined what was taxable at which rates, were often players on both the state and private side of the economy (Paneyakh, 2008). While the actual tax collection was undertaken by locally assigned bureaucrats of the central tax authority, the setting of those land rates was the responsibility of the local legislature (Timofeev, 2003).

The income tax on the other hand is federally administered at face value and set to a universal 13%, but on the ground the tax relies on employers withholding an appropriate portion of the income of their employees. This is a process rife with opportunity for misreporting as small and medium businesses throughout Russia were subject to arbitrary and corrupt local tax authorities who were often tasked from above with quotas of additional taxes to be raised, leading to a primary complaint of entrepreneurs being "the uncertainty and changeability of taxes and fines," comparing local authorities with "racketeers" (Sakaeva, 2018). Indeed, businesses are forced "to provide unofficial payments, [and] city authorities can use administrative pressure, for example, the refusal to grant licenses, operating permits." In addition, the nearly universal cash nature of wages means that employees collaborate with each other and with their employers to choose a balance between actual pay and reported pay in order to optimize the tax situations of all the parties involved. As Nancy Ries observes in this time period: "the employees (non-owners) of several businesses explained to me that they themselves decide what portion of their income to receive 'openly' and what portion to receive 'under the table'" (Ries, 1999). This process is further complicated by the fact that a portion of the collected income taxes are remitted to the regions themselves.

The mechanism I propose by which middle-and working-class shum affects tax policy in the regions is that local legislatures of this time period were caught in a tension of competing preferences. On one hand, they were responsible for maximizing revenue through the setting of local land tax rates and controlling definitions of what land was taxed at which rate locally (including via zoning and other legal controls), including land which they themselves might own. On the other hand, due to their heavy involvement with local corporations, they were also involved with the ubiquitous tax evasion associated with accurately reporting and withholding owed taxes from the wages of employees.

This complex set of preferences was mediated by the additional need within a burgeoning authoritarian regime to keep a lid on local political outcry, especially after the long chaos of the Yeltsin era. As such, local legislatures in this period would have the tools to either increase land taxes (via their legislative authority) or increase effective income tax revenue (via their members' ownership of interest in local businesses and associated arrangements with employees), setting up the strategic action that I model in the formal theory section of the article.

sections.paper_id: Wilson - 2019 - Modeling and measuring class conflict in Russia’s regions
paragraphs: Shum was a more difficult variable to construct than tax rates. On a definitional level, what we are looking for is how vocal the middle class and working class is in each Russian region. Existing measures tend to count significant events in order to measure this phenomenon, for example, counting the number of protest events in a region during a particular time period. 10 This is problematic though, because such events are not only rare (thus making it difficult to capture a measure for all regions), but are very difficult to disentangle into specific expressions of the middle and working classes independent of other political issues. This level of clamor can be measured through the attention paid to classes in regional Russian newspapers. Drawing upon Neuendorf and Skalski (2009), I implemented a combination of human and computer coding of regional Russian newspaper articles, in order to quantify the noise generated by classes in each Russian region.

One concern is that the press in Russia is hardly free in the Western sense, with extensive informal, if not formal, censorship. However, this censorship usually emerges over revelations of personal corruption or embarrassment of powerful figures, rather than touching generally upon political topics. While this is hardly conducive in general to free speech, it nevertheless does not represent a major restriction upon discussion of class issues in newspapers, which often emerge from decidedly nonpolitical sources, such as literary journals or automotive newspapers. Such coverage might be nonpolitical but is certainly salient to the magnitude of shum.

A second concern is whether content analysis of newspapers can actually measure the clamor of particular groups. An illustrative example is the early work of Pierre Bourdieu in Algeria during the 1960s.

Bourdieu observed that while political developments in Algeria seemed to follow classic Marxist predictions about the formation of a conscious proletariat and a clear dialectic, the reality was much more nuanced. Although Algerian society structurally mimicked the Marxist pattern, in actuality only a minority of Algerians were conscious of the class conflict. The masses were not a revolutionary proletariat, but rather revolution was pressed forward by a relatively small "working-class elite." Even if only a strong and vocal minority identifies with a class and generates noise in the regional presses, it can be politically relevant, and take on the form of an identity that represents the interests of a much larger structural component. 11
Theory. I operationalize shum using the following functional forms, where S M and S W are middle-and working-class shum, respectively, for a given region

where i is an index, n is the number of newspapers in that region, M i and W i are the numbers of articles appearing in newspaper i matching the middleclass or working-class search terms, C i is the circulation rate of that newspaper, and A i is the total number of articles in that newspaper. In more colloquial terms, this equation represents the total number of views of working-or middle-class articles as a proportion of the total number of articles viewed on any subject in the newspapers of each region. Or to put it in terms of shum, this represents a reasonable proxy for the level of public noise related to the working and middle classes as a proportion of the total public noise in the region.

To acquire a database of articles for analysis, I searched the Integrum database's regional Russian archive of some 1000 newspapers. The search terms were broken into two categories: middle class and working class. The middle-class category searched for the term "middle-class" (srednii klass) while the working-class category searched for "proletariat," "manual labor" (ruchnoi trud, used idiomatically for the working class), or "working-class" (rabochii klass). The searches were performed on all declined versions of these terms, and include 4 years of data from 2001 to 2004. Prior to 2001, the number of digitized records drops off drastically. Since larger papers are likely to have been digitized first, those available prior to 2001 are unlikely to be representative of the articles of the time, introducing unpredictable bias. The end result is a database of 21,250 Russian-language articles, split roughly evenly between the working and middle classes. This represents M i and W i in the equations. 12 With a combination of Public.Ru (a Russian government database of media), 13 pulling newspapers from the state archives, and checking newspaper websites, I assembled circulation figures for 97% of Russian regional newspapers. This represents C i in the above equations.

In order to generate A i , I used the same technique that found middle-and working-class articles. The most common word in the Russian language is "v," which translates roughly as "in." According to Integrum's word frequency statistics, "v" appears in 100% of articles in their database. So I simply searched for "v," and used the number of articles it appeared in within each newspaper as the total number of articles that appeared in that newspaper, thus producing A i for the equations above.

Potential concerns. There are a few interesting caveats to the data though. The federal cities of Moscow and St Petersburg are also the capitals of (and are completely surrounded by) the Moscow and Leningrad Oblasts. This leads to Moscow Oblast having 22 newspapers while Moscow proper has a mere 4. This may be an artifact of the exclusion of large national newspapers from the study (because they are read widely across the nation and cannot be regionally disaggregated). However, many national newspapers serve as the de facto regional newspaper for their home region (for instance, the New York Times). On the other hand, the federal city of St Petersburg has 92 newspapers, whereas the surrounding Leningrad Oblast only has 7. That may point to a tendency of newspapers catering to the Leningrad Oblast to headquarter for business reasons within St Petersburg proper, which leads to an overreporting of newspapers within St Petersburg at the expense of underreporting newspapers serving the larger oblast. As such, in the later empirics, I include dummy variables for these four regions.

Another potential concern is that the resultant measures of S W and S M were moderately correlated (0.45). Some correlation between S W and S M is expected from my model, but if the correlation is systematic and deriving from a hidden variable, then these figures would contain bias. However, such bias would be independent of the semantic meaning of "middle-class" and "working-class," so if such error exists, it should be present in the process regardless of what particular search terms were included in the algorithm. As such, I took two Russian words that have no semantic connection to our original search terms: chto (which translates roughly as "what" and appears in 66% of articles) and koshka (which translates as "cat" and appears in 0.5% of articles). I subjected these results to the same process as described above for middle-and working-class terms, that is, assigning the articles to regions, weighting them for circulation, and dividing by the total circulation-weighted article counts generated with "v." The cross-correlations are reported in Table 2, and demonstrate no systematic correlation between semantically unrelated words when subjected to the algorithm. This means that the correlation between S M and S W is not due to a hidden variable bias.

Finally, there is the concern that these measures are merely picking up structural attributes of the middle and working classes. That is, that middleclass shum will be larger where the middle class is larger, and that this elaborate exercise in measurement is simply picking up that variation rather than the clamor of those classes. This concern is easy to test for, by comparing the correlation of S W and S M with structural measures of the middle and working classes. I calculated the correlation of S W and S M with a variety of structural measures typically used as proxies for either the working or middle class. The results, which are displayed in Table 3, show that the measurements of shum constructed in this article have little correlation with any structural measures of class.

Content analysis. In order to empirically test the model, the problem of endogeneity between shum and taxes must be addressed, using two sets of instruments. The instruments should shift working-and middle-class shum without shifting taxes (i.e. shift S W and S M but not τ M T / . The approach I took was to identify a set of topics to which each article in the database belonged. Then I classified whether each topic was substantively related to taxation. Those articles in non-tax topics were used to construct disaggre- 2010), a Bayesian approach that sorts documents into topics based on word frequency statistics. The method's logic is language-neutral and I adapted it for use on Russianlanguage documents. The algorithm takes an array of all documents, including word counts for each distinct word across all documents, and a desired number of topics. The algorithm successively groups the documents into smaller and smaller groups, developing "ideal" word frequency counts for each topic iteratively. The end result is a posterior probability of each document belonging to each topic, along with a listing of the most statistically significant words within each topic. Because the algorithm is blind to language, the human work comes after the fact, in examining both the statistically significant word list and a random selection of articles within each topic in order to describe in human language the topic identified by the algorithm.

I wrote custom software to produce the input data for the Quinn et al. algorithm from my database of articles. The software stripped all punctuation, numbers, and other "non-words" from the articles and then converted all letters to lower case to avoid case sensitivity issues. All words in each document were then processed using a Russian language "stemmer," which is an algorithm that strips off word endings (such as "ing" and "ed" in English) so that different versions of the same word are not counted separately. The result was some 188,000 unique words. In order to make the calculation more tractable, any words that appeared in less than 2% of documents were eliminated. This yielded an input matrix of 10,554 by 4087 for the middle-class documents and an input matrix of 10,453 by 4629 for the working-class documents. As a robustness check, I also ran all the documents together with an input matrix of 21,007 by 7167. The results were relatively comparable. I ran the algorithm for a variety of different topic counts, ranging from 3 to 50. With too few topics, the topics were too vague, with the statistically significant words for each being dominated by words that seemed substantively to fit into others equally as well. With too many topics, many of the categories seemed to become noise, with many of the statistically significant words becoming content-neutral words. Although it is a subjective judgment, there seemed to be a sweet spot at about 12 topics, in which a clear delineation existed between topics while they retained their substantive significance. Tables 4 and 5 list the topics along with translations of the most significant words. The topics were labeled as "Noise" if their statistically significant words contained no substantive significance, that is, they consisted primarily of prepositions and other such words. Some such categories are expected to appear in order to capture the articles that do not properly belong to middle-and working-class topics but only used the search terms in passing. The topics were labeled as "Miscellaneous" if they contained substantively significant words, but a unifying pattern to the topic could not be discerned. Over 98% of documents in both the categories were assigned to a single topic with at least 95% confidence, while over 95% were assigned with greater than 99% confidence.

These statistically significant words allow the quick identification of topics that have a connection to taxation, leaving behind topics that should be shifters of shum but not of tax policy, and therefore be good instruments. The middle-class articles only have two topics that are clearly unrelated to taxation: education and automotive. The balance of the middle-class articles deal with topics related to economics, politics, or finance, all of which can be linked to taxes. The working-class articles have more variety, with various topics of cultural categories that would have no direct link to taxation: the world wars, holiday/vacation, working-class culture, education, and the workplace. Although the arts would at face value seem unconnected to taxation, that topic was ruled out because one of the most statistically significant words in the category was "Illyich," which suggested specific Communist connotations that might be linked to politics and taxation. Therefore, these particular instruments should theoretically be well-suited to meeting the important exclusion restriction of instrument variable usage. 14 Note that these particular findings have interesting implications beyond the use of testing regional tax policy, in that they provide a glimpse into the cultural meaning of the middle and working classes in Russia.
sections.text:
Shum was a more difficult variable to construct than tax rates. On a definitional level, what we are looking for is how vocal the middle class and working class is in each Russian region. Existing measures tend to count significant events in order to measure this phenomenon, for example, counting the number of protest events in a region during a particular time period. 10 This is problematic though, because such events are not only rare (thus making it difficult to capture a measure for all regions), but are very difficult to disentangle into specific expressions of the middle and working classes independent of other political issues. This level of clamor can be measured through the attention paid to classes in regional Russian newspapers. Drawing upon Neuendorf and Skalski (2009), I implemented a combination of human and computer coding of regional Russian newspaper articles, in order to quantify the noise generated by classes in each Russian region.

One concern is that the press in Russia is hardly free in the Western sense, with extensive informal, if not formal, censorship. However, this censorship usually emerges over revelations of personal corruption or embarrassment of powerful figures, rather than touching generally upon political topics. While this is hardly conducive in general to free speech, it nevertheless does not represent a major restriction upon discussion of class issues in newspapers, which often emerge from decidedly nonpolitical sources, such as literary journals or automotive newspapers. Such coverage might be nonpolitical but is certainly salient to the magnitude of shum.

A second concern is whether content analysis of newspapers can actually measure the clamor of particular groups. An illustrative example is the early work of Pierre Bourdieu in Algeria during the 1960s.

Bourdieu observed that while political developments in Algeria seemed to follow classic Marxist predictions about the formation of a conscious proletariat and a clear dialectic, the reality was much more nuanced. Although Algerian society structurally mimicked the Marxist pattern, in actuality only a minority of Algerians were conscious of the class conflict. The masses were not a revolutionary proletariat, but rather revolution was pressed forward by a relatively small "working-class elite." Even if only a strong and vocal minority identifies with a class and generates noise in the regional presses, it can be politically relevant, and take on the form of an identity that represents the interests of a much larger structural component. 11

Theory. I operationalize shum using the following functional forms, where S M and S W are middle-and working-class shum, respectively, for a given region

where i is an index, n is the number of newspapers in that region, M i and W i are the numbers of articles appearing in newspaper i matching the middleclass or working-class search terms, C i is the circulation rate of that newspaper, and A i is the total number of articles in that newspaper. In more colloquial terms, this equation represents the total number of views of working-or middle-class articles as a proportion of the total number of articles viewed on any subject in the newspapers of each region. Or to put it in terms of shum, this represents a reasonable proxy for the level of public noise related to the working and middle classes as a proportion of the total public noise in the region.

To acquire a database of articles for analysis, I searched the Integrum database's regional Russian archive of some 1000 newspapers. The search terms were broken into two categories: middle class and working class. The middle-class category searched for the term "middle-class" (srednii klass) while the working-class category searched for "proletariat," "manual labor" (ruchnoi trud, used idiomatically for the working class), or "working-class" (rabochii klass). The searches were performed on all declined versions of these terms, and include 4 years of data from 2001 to 2004. Prior to 2001, the number of digitized records drops off drastically. Since larger papers are likely to have been digitized first, those available prior to 2001 are unlikely to be representative of the articles of the time, introducing unpredictable bias. The end result is a database of 21,250 Russian-language articles, split roughly evenly between the working and middle classes. This represents M i and W i in the equations. 12 With a combination of Public.Ru (a Russian government database of media), 13 pulling newspapers from the state archives, and checking newspaper websites, I assembled circulation figures for 97% of Russian regional newspapers. This represents C i in the above equations.

In order to generate A i , I used the same technique that found middle-and working-class articles. The most common word in the Russian language is "v," which translates roughly as "in." According to Integrum's word frequency statistics, "v" appears in 100% of articles in their database. So I simply searched for "v," and used the number of articles it appeared in within each newspaper as the total number of articles that appeared in that newspaper, thus producing A i for the equations above.

Potential concerns. There are a few interesting caveats to the data though. The federal cities of Moscow and St Petersburg are also the capitals of (and are completely surrounded by) the Moscow and Leningrad Oblasts. This leads to Moscow Oblast having 22 newspapers while Moscow proper has a mere 4. This may be an artifact of the exclusion of large national newspapers from the study (because they are read widely across the nation and cannot be regionally disaggregated). However, many national newspapers serve as the de facto regional newspaper for their home region (for instance, the New York Times). On the other hand, the federal city of St Petersburg has 92 newspapers, whereas the surrounding Leningrad Oblast only has 7. That may point to a tendency of newspapers catering to the Leningrad Oblast to headquarter for business reasons within St Petersburg proper, which leads to an overreporting of newspapers within St Petersburg at the expense of underreporting newspapers serving the larger oblast. As such, in the later empirics, I include dummy variables for these four regions.

Another potential concern is that the resultant measures of S W and S M were moderately correlated (0.45). Some correlation between S W and S M is expected from my model, but if the correlation is systematic and deriving from a hidden variable, then these figures would contain bias. However, such bias would be independent of the semantic meaning of "middle-class" and "working-class," so if such error exists, it should be present in the process regardless of what particular search terms were included in the algorithm. As such, I took two Russian words that have no semantic connection to our original search terms: chto (which translates roughly as "what" and appears in 66% of articles) and koshka (which translates as "cat" and appears in 0.5% of articles). I subjected these results to the same process as described above for middle-and working-class terms, that is, assigning the articles to regions, weighting them for circulation, and dividing by the total circulation-weighted article counts generated with "v." The cross-correlations are reported in Table 2, and demonstrate no systematic correlation between semantically unrelated words when subjected to the algorithm. This means that the correlation between S M and S W is not due to a hidden variable bias.

Finally, there is the concern that these measures are merely picking up structural attributes of the middle and working classes. That is, that middleclass shum will be larger where the middle class is larger, and that this elaborate exercise in measurement is simply picking up that variation rather than the clamor of those classes. This concern is easy to test for, by comparing the correlation of S W and S M with structural measures of the middle and working classes. I calculated the correlation of S W and S M with a variety of structural measures typically used as proxies for either the working or middle class. The results, which are displayed in Table 3, show that the measurements of shum constructed in this article have little correlation with any structural measures of class.

paragraphs: In addition, τ M is the revenue raised from land taxes (i.e. targeting the middle class) and τ W is the revenue raised from personal income taxes (i.e. targeting the working class), with the overall proportion being the Working Class Tax Bias. On the right side of the equation, let us refer to the function of S W and S M as the Working Class Shum Bias. That is, the equation simply states that all else being equal, the higher the working class's relative shum, the more that tax collection will be biased toward the working class. 16 The first column in Table 6 displays the results of an ordinary least squares regression of Working Class Tax Bias on the exogenous variables identified above. 17 The top number in each row is the coefficient, while the number in parentheses is the standard error. The results are encouraging, yielding a highly statistically significant relationship between that tax bias and the Working Class Shum Bias. The relationship is also of a high enough magnitude to have real substantive significance. The coefficient of 6.41, when multiplied by the mean shum bias of 0.6, yields an average effect of 3.9 percentage points. The mean tax bias is 8.2 percentage points, and so the average effect of the shum bias is about half the average size of the tax bias.

With any regression analysis, concerns of the multicollinearity of the independent variables are a necessary concern, and as such, Figure 2 illustrates the correlation matrix for all of the non-dichotomous covariates. The variables that are highly correlated with each other can be grouped into two patterns. First, the three Petrov-Titkov variables are highly correlated with each other, as should be expected from a set of indicators drawn from the same study to measure different aspects of a similar overall concept. However, dropping all three indicators from the regressions (or alternately, using the single "Regional Democracy" aggregate they form with other variables in the Petrov-Titkov study) does not meaningfully change any of the regression analysis presented here. Second, urbanization rates are highly correlated with a number of different variables in predictable ways (for instance, urban areas have higher overall tax revenue, more civil society, and more independent media). However, again, removing this indicator does not appreciably change the results. Finally, it is important to note that my measure of working-class tax bias is minimally correlated with any of the control variables, further indicating that the regression analysis is not violating assumptions of independence and is teasing out the distinct effect of dueling class identities in the region. Ordinary least squares cannot account for the endogenous relationship between tax bias and the shum bias though, so I also use a two-stage least squares approach using the instruments derived in the previous section. In the first stage, I regress the shum bias on the instruments for the hassle generated by both the middle class and the working class, in addition to the vector of exogenous controls. In the second stage, I regress tax bias on the predictions of the shum bias from the first stage, in addition to the exogenous controls.

The results of this 2SLS regression are displayed in Table 6 in the second column. Once again, the effect of the shum bias is both statistically and substantively significant. With a coefficient of 12.49, multiplied by the mean shum bias of 0.6, this yields an effect of 7.5 percentage points. Since the mean tax bias is 8.2, the average effect of the shum bias is roughly the same average size as the tax bias. 18 Figure 3 illustrates the magnitude of substantive effect across the full range of observed shum bias. Note that the x-axis contains ticks for each region, which shows clearly that there is a wide distribution along the independent variable of interest that is largely normally distributed if graphed as a kernel density plot. In any case, the results are not being driven by outliers on either end of the variable's spectrum.

Examination of the regions at the extremities is illustrative to establish some face validity for the findings. Of the five highest regions in shum bias, that is, regions with the highest level of bias toward the working class in the calculation of shum, three are in Russia's Central District (Kursk, Ivanovo, and Lipetsk) while a fourth (Ulyanovsk) is on its outskirts in the Volga. This area is traditionally the heart of Russian industry, retaining extensive heavy industry even after the collapse of the 1990s, and so all else being equal we would expect a stronger working class than middle class in these areas. The 12% point shift in tax bias in those regions is reflected in the relatively high land tax (on average 3.4% of revenue vs the Russian average of 2.1%) and low income tax (19.6% of revenue vs the Russian average of 23.2%). At the other end of the scale, the five lowest working-class shum regions are dominated by the Far East (Chukotka, Primorie, and Amur), which have little industry, and the city of St Petersburg and Nizhnii Novgorod, which are historical centers of the middle class in Russia. These five regions have extremely low land tax revenue (only 1.25% of revenue) and very high income tax revenue (24.9% of revenue). Recall that the regression work does control for each region's per capita GDP and urbanization, so this is not merely an artifact of those factors.

In order to contemplate the robustness of these findings, let us consider what the statistics ought to reveal if my theory were incorrect. There are two main alternative explanations for the relationship between the shum bias and tax bias. First, consider the case in which the choice of tax policy is effectively random with regard to citizen opinion. In this case, the coefficient of the term predicted in our game should be statistically indistinguishable from zero, or if it is statistically significant, then it should not be substantively significant. Since the regression analysis reveals statistical and substantive significance, it is clear that there is some relationship between the shum bias and the subsequent tax bias.

Second, one could argue that while the bureaucrat is not affected by shum within their region, there would still be a causal effect of tax policy on our measure of the shum bias because we would expect the group against whom taxes are more biased to make more noise in the newspapers. A reasonable functional form for that in the context of our model would be a disturbance proportional to S S S M M W / ( ) +
, reflecting a relative increase in uproar by the middle class as its tax burden increased. Conveniently, this is a rough approximation of 1

, and so if this alternative explanation is correct, then the shum bias should be statistically significant, but it should have a negative sign instead of our predicted positive sign. Since the results of both OLS and 2SLS regressions yield a statistically significant positive coefficient for the shum bias, this alternative hypothesis can be ruled out as well.
