sections.paper_id: Wilson - 2019 - Modeling and measuring class conflict in Russia’s regions
paragraphs: This article has demonstrated that there is indeed the appearance of a market effect in regional Russian strategies for setting the tax policy. Although the article treated the methods of citizen pressure as a black box, it represents a valuable contribution by demonstrating empirically that even in an authoritarian system, there is still strategic interaction between ruler and ruled even in the absence of the threat of revolt. The article generates a new measure of Russian regional popular pressure, constructs a novel formal theory for how groups interact with the local authorities in an authoritarian context, and refines existing content analysis techniques. In addition, the article sets up a variety of expansions for future work to pursue, such as the expansion of the model to function on more than a single policy dimension, modeling of an arbitrary number of interest groups, and of course research into the actual methods and vectors of citizen pressure that have been demonstrated to exist by this article's empirics. Quantitative Methods Workshop at UW-Madison for feedback, Yoshiko Herrera for her social identity course that formed the conceptual basis of the article, Alexander Tahk for his Bayesian statistics class that informed the content analysis methods, Scott Gehlbach for his game theory classes that seeded the formal model, librarians at the UIUC 2013 Summer Research Lab for assistance with tax data, librarians at the National Library of Russia in St Petersburg who gave me access to newspaper circulation databases, and two anonymous reviewers for their highly constructive comments. 2. For the historically inclined, there is also a wonderful body of literature focused on Imperial Russia's nascent middle class prior to the Communist Revolution. See the edited collections Between Tsar and People (Clowes et al., 1991) and Russia's Missing Middle Class (Balzer, 1996) for a starting point. 3. This is a common assumption in formal models of intergroup conflict. For example, see Acemoglu and Robinson (2009) and Mesquita et al. (2004). 4. The role of the federal government could suggest a center-periphery model.

But the core tension of that model is in the differing preferences of center and periphery. In this context, center and periphery have identical preferences. The center wants a certain amount of revenue, with minimal protest by the population. The bureaucrat wants the same thing. As such, the strategic situation does not align with a center-periphery model. 5. If the game is instead modeled as an infinitely repeating game of perfect information, it becomes clear that so long as the bureaucrat is the last player to move in each round, the game collapses down to being identical to this partial equilibrium model. This is because the bureaucrat's utility function gives no room for defection and thus she will always purchase tax revenue so as to minimize cost in that round. Although the other two players could conceivably decide to defect (which in this context means to invest less than their equilibrium amount in shum), they would immediately be punished in the same round by paying extra taxes, with no benefit. 6. I have also used the implicit function theorem to solve for the optimum levels of S M and S W that the middle and working classes should deploy in equilibrium.

The comparative statistics of these results do not yield any surprises. However, as the functions are complicated, and I do not use them for the empirical testing of the model later in the article, I will leave them out for the sake of parsimony. 7. Data were available for all the Russian regions in 2004 with the exception of Pskov and Chita. Tax data was from Nikitichna (2006). Where available, data were verified against the official Russian Federation tax agency's archival data online at: www.nalog.ru 8. Note that the classic American dichotomy would be between land tax and sales tax, since the latter disproportionately affects lower incomes. However, the nature of sales taxes in Russia (implemented largely as excise, or valueadded taxes on alcohol, tobacco, and gasoline) make them completely out of the hands of tax collectors, and largely invisible to consumers anyway. 9. Also see the historically archived tax code of the Russian Federation available at http://base.garant.ru 10. However, the existence of class-centric protest (such as workplace protest in steel mills) in the first place in Russia during this period is useful evidence of how the population was able to mobilize for economic interests. See Evans (2018) for details. 11. See in addition, numerous articles by Rodney Benson, examining the connection between the media and power. 12. The results were almost entirely dichotomous, with less than 1% of articles (209) appearing in both the sets. One might expect that if two groups were at odds with one another, more articles would use both sets of terms, speaking positively of one group while speaking negatively of the other within the same texts. A representative reading of middle-class articles did not find any negative portrayals of the working class. However, reading of working-class articles did turn up an interesting pattern: the use of "bourgeois" (burzhuaziia) and related terms as a slur for the middle class. Of the working-class articles, burzhuaziia appeared in 8% (837). Many of these articles are reprints of old Soviet articles (often from the 1920s) or present similar arguments. 13. Access provided by the Russian National Library. 14. This can be validated by verifying that the chosen instruments are not highly correlated with the eventual dependent variable (working-class tax bias) that I discuss fully in the following section. The average correlation of these instruments with tax bias is a scant -0.06, with a range of -0.19 to 0.10. This exceptionally low correlation, when taken in conjunction with the theoretical justification for their use, indicates that the particular instruments are working correctly as instruments: shifting shum without independently shifting tax policy. 15. A wide range of different values for α and β were tested, and the results were robust so long as both were less than one. These results have been left out for the sake of parsimony. 16. For ease of interpretation, the tax bias is multiplied by 100, so that it is in percentage points on a scale of 0-100. 17. All regressions were performed in R, and the reported standard errors are robust (i.e. Hubert-White) standard errors. 18. Additional specifications of the regressions were run dropping Moscow, St Petersburg, and their client oblasts entirely from the regressions. The results do not meaningfully change.

All regressions were performed in R, and the reported standard errors are robust (i.e. Hubert-White) standard errors. 18. Additional specifications of the regressions were run dropping Moscow, St Petersburg, and their client oblasts entirely from the regressions. The results do not meaningfully change.
sections.text_with_footnotes:
This article has demonstrated that there is indeed the appearance of a market effect in regional Russian strategies for setting the tax policy. Although the article treated the methods of citizen pressure as a black box, it represents a valuable contribution by demonstrating empirically that even in an authoritarian system, there is still strategic interaction between ruler and ruled even in the absence of the threat of revolt. The article generates a new measure of Russian regional popular pressure, constructs a novel formal theory for how groups interact with the local authorities in an authoritarian context, and refines existing content analysis techniques. In addition, the article sets up a variety of expansions for future work to pursue, such as the expansion of the model to function on more than a single policy dimension, modeling of an arbitrary number of interest groups, and of course research into the actual methods and vectors of citizen pressure that have been demonstrated to exist by this article's empirics.
